name: Transfer Data to Cloud Account
description: Adversaries exfiltrate data by transferring it to a cloud account under their control. In Azure environments, this typically involves Azure Storage account copy operations (AzCopy, Blob copy), creation of Azure Snapshots exported to an attacker-controlled storage account, or use of Azure Data Factory to pipeline data to external storage. AzureActivity logs snapshot exports and cross-account copy operations. Defenders look for storage operations where the destination tenant or subscription differs from the source.
iocs: AzureActivity 'MICROSOFT.COMPUTE/SNAPSHOTS/WRITE' creating disk snapshots of production VMs by a non-backup service identity
AzureActivity 'MICROSOFT.COMPUTE/DISKS/BEGINGETACCESS/ACTION' generating SAS URLs for managed disks — enables download from external context
AzureActivity 'MICROSOFT.STORAGE/STORAGEACCOUNTS/BLOBSERVICES/CONTAINERS/BLOBS/WRITE' where the Properties show a source URL pointing to an external Azure storage account
StorageBlobLogs OperationName 'CopyBlob' with CopySource pointing to a storage account in a different subscription or tenant
AzureActivity operations creating Azure Data Factory pipelines with linked services pointing to external storage endpoints
AzureActivity 'MICROSOFT.COMPUTE/SNAPSHOTS/WRITE' followed within 30 minutes by 'MICROSOFT.COMPUTE/SNAPSHOTS/BEGINGETACCESS/ACTION' on the same resource
Sudden spike in egress bytes in Azure Monitor storage metrics coinciding with AzureActivity write operations to an unknown storage account
evasion_variants: Use an attacker-controlled Azure subscription in the same region to avoid cross-region egress alerts and reduce latency-based anomaly triggers
Stage the exfiltration through an Azure Storage account in a resource group that has a legitimate-sounding name (e.g., 'backup-rg', 'dr-storage-rg')
Use Azure managed disk export with a short-lived SAS token so the export URL is not directly attributable to the attacker's infrastructure
Create snapshots incrementally (differential snapshots) to reduce total data volume transferred and stay under threshold-based alerts
Use Azure Data Factory with a Managed Identity for the linked service connection to avoid hardcoded credentials in the pipeline configuration
Transfer data via Azure Site Recovery replication to an attacker-controlled vault — replication traffic is expected and may not be monitored
detection_kql_hints: AzureActivity | where OperationNameValue in ('MICROSOFT.COMPUTE/SNAPSHOTS/WRITE','MICROSOFT.COMPUTE/DISKS/BEGINGETACCESS/ACTION') | project TimeGenerated, Caller, CallerIpAddress, ResourceId | order by TimeGenerated asc

AzureActivity | where OperationNameValue == 'MICROSOFT.COMPUTE/SNAPSHOTS/BEGINGETACCESS/ACTION' | extend ResourceName = tostring(split(ResourceId, '/')[-1]) | join kind=inner (AzureActivity | where OperationNameValue == 'MICROSOFT.COMPUTE/SNAPSHOTS/WRITE') on ResourceName | where abs(datetime_diff('minute', TimeGenerated, TimeGenerated1)) < 60
StorageBlobLogs | where OperationName == 'CopyBlob' | where CopySource !startswith strcat('https://', AccountName) | project TimeGenerated, AccountName, CopySource, CallerIpAddress
AzureActivity | where OperationNameValue has 'DATAFACTORY' and OperationNameValue has 'WRITE' | where Caller !in (known_adf_service_principals) | project TimeGenerated, Caller, Properties
AzureActivity | where OperationNameValue == 'MICROSOFT.COMPUTE/SNAPSHOTS/WRITE' | where Caller !in (known_backup_accounts) | summarize count() by Caller, bin(TimeGenerated, 1d)
